title: Training Coordination Solutions
description: A 16-month co-op project with the Ministry of Transportation's Engagement and Advisory Branch (May–October 2024) — creating a digital registration and tracking system for employee training courses.

sections.heading: The Challenge
sections.body:
How might we help training coordinators by reducing the time-consuming, complex, and manual nature of their current workflow?

Prior systems relied on spreadsheets, emails, and multiple disconnected tools — including LearnON and separate training databases — creating fragmented workflows that were slow, confusing, and relied heavily on managers to complete simple tasks.

sections.heading: My Role
sections.body:
User Research — Led discovery workshops and interviews with Business Service Analysts (BSAs), managers, and employees.

UX Design — Designed prototypes for three distinct user types: training coordinator, employee, and manager.

Usability Testing — Facilitated three testing phases with BSAs and managers, iterating on feedback each round.

Process Improvement — Co-created a streamlined, employee-driven registration model to reduce coordinator workload.

sections.heading: Prototypes
sections.body:
Manage Courses (Training Coordinator View) — A dashboard allowing coordinators to create, manage, and track training courses across the branch.

Find & Register for Courses (Employee View) — An employee-facing interface that lets staff browse available courses and self-register, reducing reliance on managers.

View Employee Training (Manager View) — A read-only reporting view that gives managers visibility into their team's training progress without becoming a bottleneck.

title: Highway Corridor Management System
description: A live-phase contribution to the Ontario Ministry of Transportation's system for tracking and managing road work on or near highways across Ontario — May to August 2025.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The Highway Corridor Management System (HCMS) was built to make it easier for the Ontario Ministry of Transportation to manage construction and development near highways. Before HCMS, applications and reviews were handled across emails, spreadsheets, and separate systems — causing confusion and delays.

The new system brings everything into one place, making the process smoother, faster, and easier to track. It also helps ensure that all projects comply with safety standards and planning requirements.

sections.heading: My Role
sections.body:
Wireframes — Designed and updated screens based on client feedback across both desktop and mobile views.

Workshops — Presented ideas and walked developers and clients through designs in collaborative working sessions.

User Stories — Worked through DevOps to gather and post information onto wireframes to support development handoff.

sections.heading: Prototypes
sections.body:
Update a Submission — One of 9 screens created to update and track a submission. These pages are used to upload files relating to each permit. Every permit has a unique number for tracking.

Application Database — A master view used to keep track of all permits and their associated information. Users can click an application number to open the full permit detail page.

Manage Payment of Permits — A receipt page that appears after a transaction within a permit. Displays all relevant information and the total paid. Names are blurred and other details use placeholders.

title: The Citizen POA Court Experience
description: A semester-long group project to redesign the Brantford Provincial Offences Court — improving its atmosphere, navigation, and accessibility as it relocated to a new building.

sections.heading: The Challenge
sections.body: The Brantford Official Provincial Court was moving buildings and needed to be redesigned to improve the atmosphere, navigation, and accessibility for citizens attending court. A major constraint the team faced was a lack of access to specific project documentation — including guidelines, expansion goals, floor plans, and budgets — which shaped our research approach.

sections.heading: Team & My Role
sections.body:
Kaeleigh — Research, Design, and Recruitment for User Testing
Jade — Project Management, Generative Research, and Design
Abi — Design, Research, and Writing
Umar — Recruitment for User Testing
Prab — Research
Mohammed — Communication

sections.heading: Research
sections.body:
Two research methods were employed: semi-structured interviews examining court processes, people, and spaces — and secondary research through literature reviews.

Key Findings: Participants preferred a clear, linear path between the lobby, waiting area, and courtroom. Users expected check-in assistance at the main entry point. Bathrooms should be located near the lobby for accessibility. Cool tones were perceived as more professional; warm tones felt more welcoming and were generally preferred.

sections.heading: Final Design
sections.body:
Atmosphere — Warm-toned wood for walls and furniture, with cooler blue accents for balance — combining welcome with professionalism.

Navigation & Wayfinding — Clear signage and information boards throughout, with a logical linear layout guiding citizens from entry to courtroom.

Accessibility — Improved seating accessibility throughout the space, with accessible bathrooms positioned near the lobby.

Proposed Services — Mediation services, legal assistance, and victim support — though full exploration was limited by delays in receiving client documentation.

sections.heading: Reflection
sections.body: Although the project was successful overall, we weren't able to fully explore adding new services for courthouse visitors due to delays in receiving client documentation. This reinforced the importance of establishing clear lines of communication and access to project constraints early in the design process.

title: Maker Lab II – SDP2.2
description: A wearable alert bracelet designed to give deaf and hard-of-hearing individuals the freedom to move around their home without worrying about missing an important alarm.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.body:
The project addresses accessibility for deaf and hard-of-hearing individuals by creating a wearable alert device. The bracelet provides visual, auditory, and haptic (vibration) feedback when an alarm sounds — giving users the freedom to move around their home without worrying about missing something important.

The prototype successfully produced a sound with a light vibration when something was within a certain distance, though it didn't fully integrate all intended features due to challenges with component integration and finding the appropriate code.

sections.heading: Design & Build
sections.body:
Components used: Arduino Uno, breadboard, LED, wires, resistors, sensors, and a sound machine. The team encountered challenges in finding appropriate code for all components and struggled with integration between hardware elements.

Alert Feedback Modes:
- Visual — LED light response
- Auditory — sound output
- Haptic — vibration feedback

sections.heading: Future Vision
sections.body: An interactive app that allows users to connect their devices — such as their fire alarm and doorbell — to the customized bracelet. Despite the unmet initial goals, the project was a valued learning experience in hardware prototyping, accessibility design, and collaborative iteration.

title: One Guard
description: A physical keyboard guard designed to help people with physical or cognitive disabilities learn and use the keyboard — making the experience comfortable for everyone.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.body: The One Guard is a way for people to easily use the keyboard. Whether you have a physical, cognitive, or no disability — the One Guard is meant to make your experience with the keyboard nice and easy.

sections.heading: Design Process
sections.body:
The team initially used cardboard for prototyping but found it wasn't accurate and was too flimsy to be practical. They pivoted to hot glue as the primary structural material, with additional components to improve accessibility and usability.

Materials:
- Hot glue — primary structural material
- Zip ties — to attach the guard to the keyboard
- Zip lock bags — to help attach the braille elements
- Little gems — used as braille indicators
- Stickers — to add colour coding to the keys

sections.heading: Reflection
sections.body: In the end, the team was proud of what was accomplished. This project helped illustrate what it's like to work in a group where everyone brings their own skills toward a common goal — particularly when that goal centres around meaningful accessibility outcomes.
